experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not validate- which in this case, was what they wanted. The less validation of the experiment, the better- since it did not have a statistically significant decrease, they could continue to ask for zip codes and get more data with little to no effect on conversion.

experiments.title: How a direct ask via voicemail impacts donor giving behaviors
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a direct ask in a voicemail increase the the donor’s likelihood to give?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2017 - 12/05/2017
For Giving Tuesday, Buckner International wanted to test out a new channel of communication to see if we could boost donor giving. We utilized a service named SlyBroadcast that allows us to make straight to voicemail phone calls. It is a way of making a phone call to thousands of participants that would immediately show up as a missed phone call on their phone. While it is a recorded message, it would appear to be a genuine voicemail given the nature of the technology.
We had previously used SlyBroadcast for cultivation and stewardship messages to donors. This time around, we wanted to make a direct ask and request that donors go online or call back to make their Giving Tuesday gift.
We focused our audience on those that had both a phone number and email address on file. We then split this audience in half by their highest previous contribution levels to create equal segments so that we could create a treatment and control group that would not be skewed by past giving.
experiments.keyLearnings: While not statistically significant, we did see a 28% decrease in the likelihood of someone that could have received the phone call to make a gift (the call had a ~60% deliverability rate so not everyone in the treatment group would have actually received the call). Given the low level of confidence, we would not go so far as to say that the phone call necessarily hurt the chances of a gift but it definitely did not increase the likelihood. Based upon these results, we will be reserving SlyBroadcast calls for cultivation and stewardship only.

experiments.title: How the style of popup influenced year end giving
experiments.researchQuestion: What style of popup will have the biggest influence on year end giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2017 - 01/01/2018
During the last few days of December, Buckner International was running a popup across their website. Historically, we had always used an exit intent so that they would only see the ad when they were finished reading the content. However, given the importance of year end, we decided to test a standard popup that would appear when a visitor first arrived at the site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Neither of the treatments statistically validated for donation or clicks to the website. However, we did see a surprising trend. The exit intent got more clicks to the donation page than the popup did but the popup received more donations over time. This may be related to a greater number of people seeing the popup which then influenced their decision to give when they received an email later that day. This is something that will definitely need further testing downstream to validate.
For posterity-sake, here are the results of the number of clicks on the popup.

experiments.title: How Autonomy Language in a website appeal popup impacts clicks to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language in a donor appeal popup reinforcing a donor’s autonomy will increase clicks by empowering them to make an informed choice
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2024 - 03/25/2024
Buckner International had a popup on the website for visitors promoting an opportunity to give $15 to give shoes to a waiting child. We hypothesized that using language that granted the reader greater autonomy to make a choice would increase clicks on the popup by empowering them to make an informed choice.
For the Control popup, we led with a statement that the reader should know something urgent. In the treatment, we asked if we could tell them about something urgent. The button language reinforced their autonomy by saying, “Yes, I want to give shoes to a waiting child.”
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, there wasn't enough data in the experiment to validate the results. The popup was setup to only fire every two visits. Due to the traffic volume of Buckner's blog, that didn't provide enough data to validate the treatment.

experiments.title: How Urgency Language impacts clicks on a website popup during a year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency to a website popup during the year-end campaign will increase clicks by making the appeal more timely.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024
For Buckner International’s Year-End campaign, we wanted to test the impact of urgency language on a popup we were running to drive website visitors to the year-end donation page. Both popups would feature an image of a mother and child in need and invite users to make a year-end gift. The difference would be in the urgency of the appeal. The control would ask if the reader could “support a family in need this season?” The treatment stated, “Time’s running out to support a family in crisis.”
We hypothesized that the added urgency of the treatment would increase engagement by making the appeal more timely. To ensure that people didn’t see the popup every visit, we set it up to show once every two visits.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, website traffic was far too low during the campaign to validate this experiment. We had only 174 sessions that saw the popups and a total of 4 clicks. While both had a healthy CTR, there wasn't enough traffic. Future tests will need to either fire every visit and/or target higher-traffic pages on the site to be able to validate.

experiments.title: How a slide up CTA with a daily donor goal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide-up CTA with a daily donor goal will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2022 - 10/31/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They wanted to find ways of getting more visitors to become monthly donors. They had used sticky bars before and found success with these at acquiring donors from visitors to their website. They hypothesized that using a different style message, one that slid up from the bottom of the page, and that included a daily donor goal would increase donor conversion. They hoped that this message would, if not leading to more monthly donors acquired, at least lead to more donors acquired.
They used a bright yellow color design to catch the eye and a low appeal threshold of just $15/month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for just over a month, we were unable to validate an impact on donor conversion from the slide-up. Our sample size of gifts was just too small. We do however want to test micro goals presented in either sticky bars or slide up CTAs during future campaigns, particularly during seasons of high urgency when we are more likely to get enough data to validate the result.

It is possible that a transition to iDonate that took place during this test may have caused us to be unable to track donations made on the main donation page that were influenced by the popup rather than made through the popup.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar impacts clicks to the donation page from website visitors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar will increase clicks to the donation page for website visitors
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/07/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They wanted to test ways of improving visitor-to-donor conversion on their website. They hypothesized that having a sticky bar with a message from a child asking for a donation with the face of the child accompanying the message would increase donor conversion by “humanizing” the appeal.
People give to people and this would make it more of a conversational, person-to-person experience. They ran the test for the entire July matching donor campaign against their control, non “humanized’ bar.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month, we were unable to validate any impact on clicks or downstream donor conversion. While we couldn't validate a lift, the client did enjoy the interactivity of the treatment design and will continue to use it and test it on other offers and portions of their donor funnel. However, in doing so, the client is not expecting a lift in conversion from the new design.

experiments.title: The impact on donor conversion of adding language about protecting subscriber privacy to a content offer landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reassuring subscribers that their data will be kept private will reduce mental friction and increase downstream donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2022 - 09/26/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They use free content offers to acquire donors on Facebook. One of their most popular offers is 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Children in Your Life . They wanted to see if they could increase donor conversion by adding a sentence about subscriber privacy to the form.  in 2017, they tested this approach and found no increase in email conversion, however tests with other organizations found that this language would could increase downstream donor conversion.
They cloned the landing page and added this just below the form: “We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to deliver your devotion.”
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for two months there was no significant impact on downstream donor conversion by adding the privacy language to the landing page. When looking at email conversion there was also no significant difference between the control and treatment pages. While other organizations have found with their visitors that this language can increase donor conversion, the audience for this offer is not motivated either way by this additional value proposition.

experiments.title: The impact of a floating shoe icon compared to a floating heart icon on donor conversion for traffic to the website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a floating heart icon will convert as many or more donors as a floating shoes icon.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 06/17/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They’ve been testing into ways to convert more of the traffic to their website into donors. Recently, we tested the impact of a floating “shoes” icon that when clicked, led donors to their donation page to provide needy children with a pair of comfortable shoes and socks. We found that the floating icon did improve donor conversion. This led to a question about whether a heart icon leading to their general donation page would be more or less effective than the shoe icon.
We wanted to be able to drive traffic to their general page rather than just their campaign specific shoes page and hoped that this would be as effective or better than the shoes icon.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As expected, we were not able to validate any statistical difference on donor conversion between the two floating icons. The primary impact on donor conversion is not limited to a specific design, but rather the fact that there is a floating, eye-catching donation button on the page.
This allows Buckner to use a floating "heart" icon to drive traffic to the general donation page throughout the year and then use the shoes icon during high urgency campaigns raising money for shoes.

experiments.title: The Impact of Testing Two Different Devotional Offers to Buckner Visitors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by testing two competing free devotional offers, we will find the one visitors are most likely to convert on and thus acquire more email addresses.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2022 - 04/16/2022
Buckner International uses Dear Readers to promote free content offers like its devotionals. We wanted to see which free devotional offer acquired more emails. To test this, we split the traffic on their article pages and delivered Faith in Action to half of the visitors and  Messages of Peace to the other half.
We hypothesized that visitors would prefer one to the other and that we would increase email acquisition by finding the best resource.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While neither offer had a significant impact email acquisition, the Faith in Action offer increased revenue by 91% with a 96% Level of Confidence for new visitors. (Though the sample size was still lower than what we would want for the most reliable results.)
This shows how testing can result in significant conversion lift in unexpected areas. Further testing is needed to see if this significant increase in revenue and donor conversion is due to something measurably different on the donation page for this offer vs the Messages of Peace devotional donation page, or if it is something inherent in the offer itself. Additional testing should also focus on pages that get enough engagement to have a larger sample size of donations.  But in the meantime, it is clear that Buckner should use the Faith in Action as its dear reader devotional offer.

experiments.title: How revising donation page copy affected year-end giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing clarity for donation page visitors will achieve increase conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2021 - 12/30/2021
When reviewing donation page copy for a year-end campaign, we noticed the headline and intro paragraph copy did not flow conversationally. We wanted to test a new version of the page that highlighted the seasonal relevance of the offer and made a more clear reflection of the need at hand. We updated the headline to propose a question rather than a command to incorporate the concept of donor autonomy and led the copy with the urgent need rather than beginning with Scripture.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were only able to run the experiment for a brief portion of the campaign and during that limited window, we did not reach the minimum number of transactions to be able to thoroughly analyze the results. We only reached 50% level of confidence so we will need to re-test this theory on a future campaign.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing additional clarity for donation page visitors will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2020 - 07/29/2020
We saw a low conversion rate on an instant donation page following a high-value offer (a free 30 day devotional eBook), so we created a treatment to the donation page. While the control copy included relevant Scripture and had ordered steps that were clear to follow, we realized it did not make a clear enough or strong enough ask. The copy was heavily centered around getting the donor to say “yes” to specific prayer requests, and the final call to action was “if you’d like to make a gift today,” which did not indicate urgency or need. The treatment copy was written to introduce the felt needs that people were experiencing and give more details about why a potential donor’s support was needed to produce resources like this devotional eBook. We also put a tangible, specific ask in the treatment copy outlining exactly what a gift of a certain amount could accomplish: “Your gift of $50 will allow us to get this resource into the hands of 20 more people who are in need…” and added two clear calls to action “Will you make a gift” and “Please make a gift” to prompt the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, the experiment did not receive enough traffic to validate. While we did not reach statistical significance, we did have a few observations to note. We saw a 90% increase in conversion rate with a 72.6% level of confidence. On the flip side, even though the treatment copy asks very specifically for a gift of $50, we saw a sharp drop in the average gift on the treatment page. Additionally, mobile visitors had a 2.3% conversion rate while desktop visitors were converting at a 9.1%, so additional tests to optimize this page specifically for mobile traffic could be explored moving forward.

experiments.title: How adding trustmarks to a donation page affected gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an evidential trustmark for donation page visitors will achieve a increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2021 - 03/14/2021
Buckner had a “100/100” Charity Navigator score so we wanted to test if adding the “Give With Confidence” trustmark to one of their donation pages would produce a lift in conversion. We rolled out the image as a form of social proof at the bottom of the page near the final decision-making point beneath the “Give” button to provide extra support through evidential proof for site visitors at the time of their gift that this is a trustworthy organization to help them be more confident in their decision.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we did not get enough traffic to this donation page to reach any conclusions about the impact of adding the trustmark to the page. The 14.7% lift in conversion rate was only supported by a 50.7% level of confidence so in reality it's still a coin-flip as to whether or not the conversion rate would increase or decrease on the treatment. We'll look for more high-volume appeals and donation pages to re-test this concept. One interesting takeaway is that we did note that the level of confidence was considerably higher for new visitors, so instead of a housefile appeal, we'll test this in acquisition next.

experiments.title: How using communal language in a sticky bar impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding communal language for website visitors will achieve increased traffic to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2021
To highlight a Challenge Grant with an upcoming deadline, we added a sticky bar across Buckner’s website to draw additional clicks over to the donation page. For the control we used time-focused language to build urgency but we wanted to test incorporating communal language and remind visitors of their friendship with the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: We ran the test for two weeks ahead of the Challenge Grant deadline but we did not receive enough traffic to validate the experiment and only reached a 55% level of confidence so we can't say for sure if this had any impact on donation page traffic. We'll look for additional opportunities to test into using communal language on housefile appeals where we have an established connection with the audience.

experiments.title: How the value proposition of the donation page impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will have the greatest impact on donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/22/2016 - 01/02/2017
Neither of the value propositions showed a statistically significant improvement over the other. With such a low instant donor conversion, is showing that the visitors were not highly motivated to give which makes finding the right language more difficult. The email offer is still receiving emails for a decent cost so we will try testing a new value proposition to improve donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: When launching the free eBook offer for The Jesus Agenda, we wanted to determine the right value proposition to use for the instant donor conversion opportunity. The initial thought was that the new subscribers may be motivated enough to make a gift to get the full version of the book. However, we also wanted to see if explaining the various aspects of Buckner’s ministry would increase the perceived value of the contribution enough to lift giving.

experiments.title: How we used A/A testing to validate the fix to an email acquisition form
experiments.researchQuestion: Is the conversion issue fixed for the NetCommunity landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/19/2016 - 09/22/2016
At the launch of The Jesus Agenda eBook offer for Buckner International, we noticed a significantly low conversion rate that did not align with what we would expect for this kind of offer. After running a series of experiments, we did not see the kind of lift we were expecting. Not wanting to give up on the offer, we decided to launch an A/A test by created an exact duplicate of the email acquisition page in a different system. After launching this test, we noticed that the normal system was seeing a significantly lower conversion rate than the one set up in the other system.
After further investigation in Google Analytics, we discovered that a security error was being thrown for Android devices. We made a fix to the page and then relaunched this test to validate that it had fixed the issue.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 4 days, this A/A test did not show a significant difference in conversion between the two systems. Based upon this result, we concluded the security warning had been the cause of the conversion problem and that it was now resolved.

experiments.title: How a radical donation page treatment impacts acquisition revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the donation page increase overall revenue to the acquisition campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2019 - 08/03/2019
After running for nearly six months, we had not been able to achieve a statistically significant lift to the donation page used with Buckner’s 7 Scripture offer. After multiple iterative approaches, we decided to test a radical redesign of the page. We combined multiple factors based upon positive results seen with similar organizations.
The down side to this approach is that it makes it difficult to isolate exactly what helped move the needle if a lift is achieved. However, the overall goal was to lift revenue and if that could be achieved then knowing the exact reason for the lift became less important.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach a statistical level of significance, we did see an increased average gift from the treatment version. Initial results saw the treatment also have a higher conversion rate but this balanced out after a few weeks of running. This is a good reminder of why it is so critical to ensure there is both a significant amount of time and total conversions before making a call on any experiment.
Our current hypothesis for the increase in average gift is that the treatment makes a targeted ask of $50 while the control version asks for $25. However, given all of the other factors that changed, we will need to isolate this factor to prove it out in the future.

experiments.title: How an updated value proposition impacted conversion on the primary donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a value proposition that focuses on children instead of the whole family and that incorporates scripture increase overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2019 - 05/02/2019
The text used on Buckner’s main donation page was created over three years ago and used messaging and tactics that we had long ago tested out of in our acquisition efforts. In an effort to boost the conversion to the page, we decided to test out an updated value proposition that incorporated scripture and focused more on the tangible impact to the children that Buckner serves instead of the holistic care being provided to all individuals.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly 2 months, we did not see any significant change in donor conversion from the treatment version of the donation page. It is possible that we did not identify the right value proposition for this audience. However, it is our hypothesis that it is likely the form itself that is causing a donor conversion rate lower than we would prefer. The next step in testing this hypothesis is setting up a new experiment that reduces the friction and anxiety associated with the form.

experiments.title: How a cultivation email prior to an appeal impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will long-form story emails positively impact overall giving to a campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2019 - 05/05/2019
In an analysis of prior appeals, Buckner International had discovered that their long story emails tended to be the lowest performing appeals. However, we had a hypothesis that while these emails may not perform well immediately, they may be influencing downstream giving. Given the time and effort required to write these emails, we decided to run a test to measure the impact of these “cultivation” style appeals when they are a part of a larger campaign.
To do this, we split the file in half and sent one segment a long story prior to the two appeal emails. The treatment just received the two appeal emails. We then monitored the donor giving across the entire campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: At the end of the campaign, we did not see any significant difference in donor conversion with the additional email. However, we did see a 50% increase to unsubscribes (with 100% level of statistical confidence) due to the addition of the extra email. Given that the additional story email did not have the positive impact on giving that we hypothesized, it would seem that we can reduce the number of emails in the appeal while negatively impacting giving.

experiments.title: How the emphasis on the power of prayer impacted sign up for a devotional
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shifting the benefits of the offer from their child to themselves increase the visitor’s likelihood to convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2019 - 03/21/2019
Buckner’s 7 Scripture offer had been running for nearly two years as a tool for email acquisition. The landing page focused on the need and benefits of this offer on the reader’s children. Based upon results we had observed at the ad-level, we had a hypothesis that we could see increased results by modifying the landing page to focus on the benefits to the reader. We also wanted to emphasize the power of prayer to reinforce the kind of good it can do in the individual’s life.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for nearly a month and a half, we did not see any statistically significant difference between the control and treatment. It would appear, that shifting the focus entirely to the reader may have too far removed the primary purpose of the offer. As a result, we ended up not seeing any change in conversion. A next natural step would be a blending of these two treatments. By positioning the offer so that it applies to both the visitor and their child, we can potentially get the best of both world.

experiments.title: How a mid-dollar premium impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a $50 premium increase overall revenue to the page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2019
The instant donation page for Buckner’s 7 Scripture offer had seen a significant increase in conversion with the implementation of Purchase targeting on Facebook and the switch to a Shoes for Orphan Souls as the donation ask. However, with the change to Shoes, we saw our average gift hovering around $25 (the cost to provide a pair of shoes.) In an effort to increase this average gift, we decided to run a test of offering a premium for a gift of $50 or more. Our belief is that by positioning this $50 ask as the first number they see, we would be able to increase overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a month, we saw that the two pages had nearly identical conversion rates and the premium offer had a slightly higher average gift. However, this average gift was something that began trailing off over time for the treatment. Our hypothesis is that the premium (a calendar) became less relevant the further we got into the year. With the offer being less relevant, people started giving $10 gifts instead of the $50 which brought the average down to a similar level as the control.
A big takeaway from this experiment is that we were able to lift average gift when the premium is both relevant and timely without negatively impacting overall donor conversion.

experiments.title: The impact of Scripture in an appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using scripture in an appeal increase donor response rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2019
Buckner International had seen success with recent communications by leaning heavily into scripture. As a Christian organization, they would incorporate scripture verses into their cultivation emails and even had an acquisition offer that focused on scripture. With this in mind, we had the hypothesis that using scriptures in our appeals may increase the reader’s sense of duty which would then increase the likelihood to respond.
However, we wanted to be sure it wasn’t isolated to a single email. To do this, we split the house file equally among donors and nondonors and maintained this split through the whole campaign. We then three distinct emails, each with a scripture and non-scripture, version to measure the impact on the gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After sending three different emails with both scripture and non-scripture versions, we did not see any statistically significant increase in donor conversion. In fact, the only email that showed any increase in giving was the initial email sent to the file (shown above). It is also worth pointing out that the control emails did not omit mentions of God, they simply did not directly incorporate scripture.
This is a fascinating learning as it tells us that we do not need to directly reference scripture in order to invoke the donors’ sense of duty.

experiments.title: How budget strategy in Facebook affects instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does Facebook’s Budget Optimization tool create more opportunities to get instant donors than setting the budget at the ad set level?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2019 - 02/11/2019
Recent experiments had resulted in a significant improvement in Buckner’s active acquisition campaign known as “7 Scriptures.” The greatest lift was the result of technical optimization of the Facebook ads. With this in mind, we wanted to take this one step further and continue our testing of the various advertising techniques available to us through Facebook.
One of the ways ads are shown is based upon the method of bidding. Historically, we had set daily spending limits for each audience which equally spread the spend across each one. We had the hypothesis that if we allowed Facebook’s algorithm to determine the allocation of spend, we may see better results. To test this, we set the budget at the campaign level, using Facebook’s Budget Optimization tool instead of the ad set level (setting a daily limit per ad set). All ads and targeting remained the same.
At the time of the test, each campaign was spending the same amount per day ($83 and change).
experiments.keyLearnings: This test was not statistically valid, but the campaign that had daily limits set for each ad set did slightly increase instant donor conversion in the time span of the test. After further discussion, we may believe that setting daily limits for the Budget Optimization was not the best route since it still limited Facebook’s algorithm to daily amounts. Instead, we are going to next test setting a campaign spending limit that allows for the media to be spent whenever the algorithm determines it is best. This would potentially allow for increased spending on days that show increased conversion opportunity.

experiments.title: How signifying a recommended gift amount affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does signifying a recommended gift amount and putting a value to that amount affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2019 - 01/31/2019
Buckner International was running their 7 Scriptures campaign and wanted to test if signifying the “most popular” donation amount, along with what that gift amount provides for a child, would affect donor conversion. They kept all copy  the same, and put a box and gift handle on the $50 amount.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment did not validate or produce any specific learnings. We’ve seen suggested gift amounts and “most popular” gifts work in the past but, in this situation, it would appear new donors were not as impacted by these suggestions. The next iteration of this experiment is to potentially increase average gift amounts by testing a premium with a minimum gift amount.

experiments.title: How the donation selection area can communicate tangible impact
experiments.researchQuestion: Will variable text around the donation amount field better communicate the impact of larger gifts and thus, increase revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2018 - 09/07/2018
For Buckner International’s Shoes for Orphan Souls fundraising campaign, we wanted to find the best way to visualize the kind of impact a gift will have. In this situation, $25 would fund a pair of shoes for a child in need. Historically, we had placed text on the donation buttons but the buttons were usually far lower than the average gift amount.
We had the hypothesis that if we removed the buttons all together and created variable copy around the textfield that visitors entered their information, we would be able to secure larger gifts. We decided to test this out during the fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for the entire campaign, we saw there was no statistically significant difference between either of the two forms with regards to conversion rate or revenue. It it likely that both treatments adequately communicated the impact of lower dollar gifts (less than $100) and that the larger dollar gifts are not as concerned about the tangible impact of their gift. They are giving because they believe in the overall mission.

experiments.title: How utilizing statistics in an email appeal influenced results
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of statistics influence the results of an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/27/2018
Buckner International was sending out their 2018 calendar year end campaign focused on the impact and need behind foster care and adoption. When discussing as a team, there was debate surrounding the use of statistics in the email appeals. There was published research indicating that statistics in an appeal could potentially lower response rates. We decided to test this theory with an email during the year end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that there was no statistically significant difference between either of the treatments with regards to both donations and clicks. It is worth noting that this is just one attempt at validating this theory. We would need to test it a few more times to truly understand the impact of incorporating statistics into our appeals.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky bar to a website affects email acquisition conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a sticky bar to our website with a content offer convert names from our web traffic?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2018 - 02/19/2018
Buckner was wanting to diversify the ways they acquired emails through content offers, and to take advantage of their website traffic to convert names. They wanted to test whether offering the 7 Scriptures offer on their website as a sticky bar popup would convert people. The initial test measured clicks to the landing page and the tested variable was control copy vs. supporter copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 10 days of running the experiment, we only acquired 1 new name from the landing page. Because of this learning, we have concluded that other forms of acquisition are more effective than a sticky bar on the webpage.

experiments.title: How aligning the value proposition to the offer influenced donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the application of Construal Level Theory increase instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2018 - 12/21/2018
For the instant donor conversion page on Buckner’s Advent Devotional, we wanted to test out a theory in conversion optimization known as Construal Level Theory. The theory states that an individual would be more likely to take an action that went against their own personal self interests if they felt it benefited a small group that they were a part of. In this situation, we were asking them to give a donation because they were part of a select group of people that wanted to protect vulnerable children this Christmas season.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this situation, we simply ran out of time before we could observe any significant influence of the treatment. We did not have enough visitors to the two variants to be able to measure the impact. Since this offer was only applicable during the Advent season, we had to turn if off two weeks into the experiment.
This is a theory worth retesting in the future when we have an offer that will see significant traffic volume or one that is being sent out to the email file.

experiments.title: How scripture and emphasizing offer importance impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will incorporating scripture and emphasizing why the offer is important increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/09/2018 - 11/16/2018
In an effort to increase signups to their Parenting Video Series, Buckner decided to try a radical approach of putting a “Parenting Pledge” prior to the Video Series acquisition page. Visitors were asked to pledge to raise their children according to scripture and were then sent to a page with the video series that could help them do that.
After launching this pledge, we noticed that the conversion rate on the page was lower than we would like it to be. To try to improve that, we decided to test out a different approach with the pledge. We rewrote the copy to incorporate scripture and to focus on why signing the pledge is important. We then tested the original pledge against this new version to see which would acquire more emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a week, we did not see any statistically significant change between the two versions. It would appear that the incorporation of scripture and emphasizing why the offer was important was not important for the page. These were two elements that were included in the advertising; it would appear that using them in the ads caused them to be unnecessary on the page.
Given the high cost for acquisition, we decided to turn off this offer and focus on other means to acquire potential donors for Buckner.

experiments.title: How simplifying a landing page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does simplifying the copy on a page bring clarity to the value proposition and increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2018 - 07/05/2018
Buckner International was offering a free parenting series course and wanted to try to increase conversions on the landing page. They hypothesized that simplifying the landing page by taking out some of the copy and clarifying the value proposition would increase conversion. They also took out many of the Buckner references and focused more on the parent the content was speaking to.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the experiment produced a 5% directional lift, there was no statistically valid learnings from this experiment. Since the page was already pretty highly converting once people got to the page, we recommend testing other parts of the funnel to significantly alter conversion rate and cost.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition about a matching gift affects click rate in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: Does additional value prop about a matching gift increase clickthrough?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2018
Buckner International was running their first June solicitation email regarding a matching gift and the program it affected. They wanted to see if adding additional value prop about the matching gift would motivate more people to click the donation link.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test was not statistically significant and did not produce a significant directional lift. If this test were to be repeated, more elements would need to be changed, or copy would need to be changed more drastically in order to see a true difference.

experiments.title: How adding an impact-focused value proposition to a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more impactful value proposition move more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/30/2018
Buckner International was running a test on the 7 Scriptures donation page to test the effectiveness of a broader impact-focused value proposition on the donation page, which encouraged the reader to provide God’s Word to a child to help them grow closer to the Lord. The control copy focused more on providing for those children’s physical and emotional needs.
experiments.keyLearnings: Despite our best efforts, this experiment didn’t move the needle enough to be statistically valid. Though this experiment was not statistically valid, we should continue to test people’s motivation for providing help not just for children’s physical and emotional needs, but their relationship with God as well.

experiments.title: How an impact-focused value proposition on a donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more impactful value proposition move people to give more generously?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2018 - 06/02/2018
Buckner International was running a test on the 7 Scriptures donation page to test the effectiveness of a broader impact-focused value proposition on the donation page, which encouraged the reader to provide God’s Word to a child to help them grow closer to the Lord. The control copy focused more on providing for those children’s physical and emotional needs.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though this experiment was not statistically valid, we did see a directional lift of 85% in average gift amount, which shows us that people are motivated by providing help not just for children’s physical and emotional needs, but their relationship with God as well. We should continue testing this type of value proposition in future messaging to see if this continues to be the case.

experiments.title: How including the brand name impacts email opens
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including the organization’s name in the sender information increase email opens?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2018
As part of a stewardship series for recent donors, Buckner was sending an email that included some stories of people that were impacted by the ministries. This email was going to donors from the last 12 months. We decided to run a quick test to see if including the organization’s name in the sender information would increase brand familiarity and, thus, boost open rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw no statistically significant difference in the sender that incorporated “Buckner International” into the sender. This tells us the brand doesn’t necessarily boost engagement but it also doesn’t get in the way.
We always strive to humanize the marketing emails that get sent out but, at the same time, we have to try different techniques to increase subscriber engagement. That is why experiments like this are important with “minor” sends like this. We can use these messages to determine what works best for the audience and can then apply it to more critical emails like those that are a part of fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How showing impact of a gift on a donation page affects revenue per donor
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the layout of the donation page affect donor conversion, and revenue per donor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/06/2017 - 12/05/2017
Buckner International wanted to increase instant donations on their 7 Scriptures acquisition offer, and wanted to test how redesigning the donation page would affect donor conversion- and also if people would give bigger gifts if they more clearly understood the impact.
The elements tested were the design of the confirmation of offer download and how it flowed into the body copy, as well as a rewritten headline and some body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this experiment did not validate at a level of confidence of 95%, we saw a trend towards higher donor conversion and higher revenue per visitor with the redesigned page. This is likely due to a more user-friendly format as well as a clearer value proposition in the headline and body copy on the page.

experiments.title: How an offer with low appeal affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will receive the highest amount of revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/09/2016 - 08/19/2016
During Buckner’s Shoes for Orphan’s Souls campaign, we ran a series of experiments with the goal of boosting donor conversion. One of the experiments was to run Facebook ads asking directly for a gift to the campaign. The normal flow for Facebook acquisition is to offer a premium in exchange for an email address followed by a donation ask. However, with this specific campaign, we thought a gift may be an appealing enough offer that the initial email acquisition was not necessary.
To make sure we got the most out of this campaign, we created two landing page treatments to test for the offer. The goal was to ensure we were going with the right message for the audience. The control included some additional language around Buckner International and the broader mission while the treatment focused specifically on the Shoes aspect of the ministry.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this experiment spoke more towards the general appeal of the offer than the individual results of the pages. After spending $200 on ads, we did not net a single donor. As a result, we shut down the offer before we could achieve a valid sample size and result. This concept, “general Buckner value prop” vs. “Shoes specific value prop” is a concept worth testing again next August.

experiments.title: How 3rd party quotes impacted the email acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will positive reviews of the book increase the appeal of the offer and, subsequently, the conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/22/2016 - 12/23/2016
As part of the promotion for their free eBook offer, Buckner International wanted to determine if adding book reviews increased the appeal of the offer. We were able to pull quotes from the book’s Amazon page and add them directly on to the page before the form.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a full 2 months, we did not see any statistically significant impact of the reviews. The page was converting at a ~40% conversion rate and a small adjustment like this did not seem to have any influence on the visitor’s likelihood to submit their information.

experiments.title: How the imagery of the page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a flat image or an eCard video produce the best email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2016 - 12/23/2016
As part of the December acquisition campaign, Buckner International was launching a dedicated eCard to be sent out to all foster and adoptive families. We then launched Facebook ads to collect “signatures” for the card. During our initial conversations, we had a debate on the best way to show the “card” that they would be signing. Initially, it was believed that a flat image would be the least distracting but then the hypothesis of a video was raised since it was believed that it would be more engaging.
experiments.keyLearnings: Since this offer had a limited time period that it could be reasonably run, we were not able to validate the experiment. However, it appears that the animated eCard video produced a lift over the flat card. It would have taken several hundred more visitors to statistically validate this result but this is definitely worth remembering for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition copy to a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does added value proposition copy on a donation page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2017 - 07/10/2017
Though this experiment did not validate, they experienced a 10% lift in donations from the added treatment copy. This could be due to a more readable format (a bulleted list) for people to make a decision to donate, or perceived added value of their donation to the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: Buckner International released a new offer giving parents a series of prayers to pray over their children. They wanted to test if adding additional value proposition and a bulleted list of what Buckner does to the donation page would increase donations. The form and other elements remained the same.

experiments.title: How transcribing a video on a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the transcription of a video convert more donors than a video alone?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 01/03/2018
Buckner International was running a video ad on Facebook targeting their donors and housefile, with a video of Dr. Reyes explaining the impact that their gifts had in 2017 on children and families that Buckner served, and inviting them to make a year-end gift.
The video linked to a landing page with the video on it and the opportunity to make a gift. They wanted to test whether transcribing Dr. Reyes’ message in the video on the page would have a greater impact than just re-watching the video.
Other elements of the donation page, including the form, remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The motivation of visitors to the page and traffic to the page was too low to make any definitive assessment of which was the better treatment. This makes sense given the reason they clicked to the page (either to watch more of the video or for more information).
It’s likely that not enough people were motivated to give because of the source of the traffic (Facebook) which has traditionally been harder to convert with direct ask ads. Also, people may have been confused by the lack of context on the donation page that only showed the video, without supporting text.
The concept of testing video vs. text treatments should be re-tried with a page that gets higher traffic to guarantee a more valid learning.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy policy to a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding privacy language to a landing page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2017 - 08/22/2017
Buckner International was running an email acquisition campaign with their 7 Scriptures offer. They wanted to see if adding privacy language around the conversion form would increase conversion rate due to a higher perceived sense of security for downloaders. All other elements of the landing page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that conversion rate stayed pretty constant between the two landing pages, with the privacy policy page showing little to no difference in conversion rate. It’s possible that a privacy policy does affect people’s perception of the security of the offer, but not having one does not deter people from downloading either.

experiments.title: How more concise value proposition affects email conversion rate on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more concise value proposition increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2017 - 09/25/2017
Buckner International was running a test on an acquisition offer that offered free scriptures to pray for your kids. They wanted to test if a shortened, more concise value proposition on the landing page would increase conversion rate. All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not validate, and we saw only a minor decrease in conversion rate from the shortened copy. This could be tested further with a more radical test to figure out if people are deterred by length of copy, or more encouraged to download with a more built-out value proposition for the offer.

experiments.title: How urgency on a donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does increasing urgency on a donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2017 - 09/29/2017
Buckner was testing more urgent copy for an immediate need on the donation page of their content offer, 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Child in your Life. The immediate need focused around Hurricane Harvey and the Buckner staff and families affected by the disaster. The ads and acquisition page for the offer remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not reach 95% validation or reach statistical power of over 80%, so the test results were not valid. However, we did see a trend towards a higher gift amount on the treatment page, likely due to the urgency of the need. It is also likely that the time window for donations to Harvey had passed once the treatment began, which could have had some effect on conversion rate.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding value proposition on a donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/12/2017 - 11/06/2017
FamilyLife was running a test on their main donation page that tested the value proposition in the body copy of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a significant or valid lift in conversion from the updated value proposition, so this test will need to be run again when there is a lot of traffic continuously to the page to validate the learning.

experiments.title: How adding region-specific wording to an email affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does making copy more personal to the reader increase click rate in an email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2017 - 11/28/2017
Buckner International was giving a free offer to people in their email file as a gift for Giving Tuesday. They wanted to test whether adding region-specific copy in the email would increase clickthrough rate. The control had generic language, whereas the treatment would use contact-specific wording and apply their most local Buckner branch’s name in that copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: The region-specific copy increased clicks by 3%, however, this experiment was not valid. A more radical test should be performed to increase learnings about conditional copy to the reader.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array to a year end donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does adding gift array buttons to a donation form affect donor conversion and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 01/03/2018
Buckner International was running their year-end campaign and wanted to test how adding a gift array to their year-end donation page would affect donor conversion and gift value. All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a 10% decrease to conversion rate (not statistically significant) but a 42% increase to revenue per visitor when we removed the buttons during year end (not statistically significant). Given the low significance, this is something that would need to be re-tested in future appeals before making any definitive statements. However, based upon these results, it may make sense to segment high dollar donors ($100+) and provide them with a different giving experience than lower level and non-donors. Basically, use the buttons to remove friction for the less motivated audience and then allow the higher donors to choose their own level of generosity. Again, this is a hypothesis that will need to be revalidated with a future test.
